headline: Rebound in tech shares pushes Asian shares higher, while oil prices fall
summary: Shares were mostly higher Thursday in Asia, led by tech-driven gains in Japan and South Korea as major computer chipmakers’ stocks surged following upbeat earnings reports from U.S. giants like Qualcomm and Micron Technology.  Oil prices fell more than $1, bringing them closer to where they were before the war with Iran started.  Qualcomm’s share price surged 12% in afterhours trading after the company announced it had raised its forecast for revenue this year to $40 billion from $22 billion.

content.title: Rebound in tech shares pushes Asian shares higher, while oil prices fall
content.summary: Shares were mostly higher Thursday in Asia, led by tech-driven gains in Japan and South Korea as major computer chipmakers’ stocks surged following upbeat earnings reports from U.S. giants like Qualcomm and Micron Technology.  Oil prices fell more than $1, bringing them closer to where they were before the war with Iran started.  Qualcomm’s share price surged 12% in afterhours trading after the company announced it had raised its forecast for revenue this year to $40 billion from $22 billion.

headline: WhatsApp's pick of Indian fintech founder signals scale of payment ambitions
summary: Kunal Shah, an Indian fintech founder with no engineering degree or Silicon Valley pedigree, has spent two decades building businesses around digital payments and consumer behaviour in India.  He now takes charge of Meta's WhatsApp as the company looks to ‌leverage the reach of the world's largest messaging platform and build a "superapp" to capture a bigger share of surging online payments, industry players say.  Meta was looking for ‌a leader with "an intuitive grasp of the immense, global product potential for WhatsApp", Chief Product Officer Chris Cox wrote in an internal memo announcing 47-year-old Shah's appointment on Monday.

content.title: WhatsApp's pick of Indian fintech founder signals scale of payment ambitions
content.summary: Kunal Shah, an Indian fintech founder with no engineering degree or Silicon Valley pedigree, has spent two decades building businesses around digital payments and consumer behaviour in India.  He now takes charge of Meta's WhatsApp as the company looks to ‌leverage the reach of the world's largest messaging platform and build a "superapp" to capture a bigger share of surging online payments, industry players say.  Meta was looking for ‌a leader with "an intuitive grasp of the immense, global product potential for WhatsApp", Chief Product Officer Chris Cox wrote in an internal memo announcing 47-year-old Shah's appointment on Monday.

headline: Global chip stocks surge as blowout Micron results reignite AI rally
summary: Global chip stocks surged on Thursday after Micron Technology's blockbuster results reignited the AI-driven rally, as investors grew more confident about persistent demand and tightening supply. Micron, a key supplier for Nvidia's AI chipsets ‌alongside South Korean chipmakers, surged more than 17% in U.S. premarket trading after it forecast quarterly profit and revenue well above expectations. The ‌company, whose shares have more than tripled in value so far this year, also said its customers had committed $22 billion to lock in supplies of memory chips, underscoring how AI-driven ​demand is tightening the market.

headline: Micron overtakes Meta, Tesla in market value amid AI-driven demand
summary:
STORY: :: Micron

Micron Technology briefly blew past the market value of Big Tech brethren Meta and Tesla for the first time ever on Thursday.

Micron's stock surge came a day after the memory chip maker - and major supplier to AI powerhouse Nvidia - forecast quarterly profit and revenue well above Wall Street's expectations. 

:: Micron

The company's shares, which have more than tripled in value so far this year, at one point soared more than 19% on Thursday, giving Micron a market cap of about $1.4 trillion.

That's roughly ten times higher than Micron's market cap just one year ago.

The company is the only U.S.-based manufacturer of high bandwidth memory chips used alongside Nvidia's AI processors.

Micron in its earnings results on Wednesday said customers such as Nvidia had committed a total of $22 billion to lock in supplies of its memory chips.

That follows in the footsteps of similar deals made by Korean rivals SK Hynix and Samsung.

The agreements are key to winning over investors who have been wary of the AI boom's durability.

Earlier this week, memory stocks led a more than $1 trillion stock rout stoked in part by valuation concerns.

:: Archive

Micron's results, however, sparked a global chip stock rally on Thursday, as investors grew more confident about persistent demand and tightening supply.

content.title: Micron overtakes Meta, Tesla in market value amid AI-driven demand
content.summary:
STORY: :: Micron

Micron Technology briefly blew past the market value of Big Tech brethren Meta and Tesla for the first time ever on Thursday.

Micron's stock surge came a day after the memory chip maker - and major supplier to AI powerhouse Nvidia - forecast quarterly profit and revenue well above Wall Street's expectations. 

:: Micron

The company's shares, which have more than tripled in value so far this year, at one point soared more than 19% on Thursday, giving Micron a market cap of about $1.4 trillion.

That's roughly ten times higher than Micron's market cap just one year ago.

The company is the only U.S.-based manufacturer of high bandwidth memory chips used alongside Nvidia's AI processors.

Micron in its earnings results on Wednesday said customers such as Nvidia had committed a total of $22 billion to lock in supplies of its memory chips.

That follows in the footsteps of similar deals made by Korean rivals SK Hynix and Samsung.

The agreements are key to winning over investors who have been wary of the AI boom's durability.

Earlier this week, memory stocks led a more than $1 trillion stock rout stoked in part by valuation concerns.

:: Archive

Micron's results, however, sparked a global chip stock rally on Thursday, as investors grew more confident about persistent demand and tightening supply.

headline: Microsoft Stock Is Having Its Worst Start to a Year Since the Dot-Com Crash
summary: Heading into Thursday’s session, Microsoft stock had fallen more than 24% in 2026, putting it on track for its steepest first-half decline since a nearly 32% drop in 2000, according to Dow Jones Market Data.  For much of its history, Microsoft was best known as the pioneer behind the Windows operating system and wildly popular Office software.  Microsoft seemed like an obvious early winner in AI: The company became OpenAI’s first major corporate backer through a $1 billion investment in 2019, aligning itself early on with one of the industry’s juggernauts.

content.title: Microsoft Stock Is Having Its Worst Start to a Year Since the Dot-Com Crash
content.summary: Heading into Thursday’s session, Microsoft stock had fallen more than 24% in 2026, putting it on track for its steepest first-half decline since a nearly 32% drop in 2000, according to Dow Jones Market Data.  For much of its history, Microsoft was best known as the pioneer behind the Windows operating system and wildly popular Office software.  Microsoft seemed like an obvious early winner in AI: The company became OpenAI’s first major corporate backer through a $1 billion investment in 2019, aligning itself early on with one of the industry’s juggernauts.

headline: Microsoft Stock Is Having Its Worst Start to a Year Since the Dot-Com Crash
summary: Heading into Thursday’s session, Microsoft stock had fallen more than 24% in 2026, putting it on track for its steepest first-half decline since a nearly 32% drop in 2000, according to Dow Jones Market Data.  For much of its history, Microsoft was best known as the pioneer behind the Windows operating system and wildly popular Office software.  Microsoft seemed like an obvious early winner in AI: The company became OpenAI’s first major corporate backer through a $1 billion investment in 2019, aligning itself early on with one of the industry’s juggernauts.

headline: Nvidia Stock Slips Below $200. Competition Fears Are Growing.
summary: Nvidia  slid on Thursday as the initial boost from  Micron’s  blowout earnings report faded, giving way to deeper anxieties about artificial-intelligence stocks.  Extending its longest losing streak since May, Nvidia stock fell for the fourth day straight, dropping 1.8% to $195.47.  As Barron’s has written, Nvidia looked to be establishing a trading range with a floor of roughly $200, after breaking through that level in April.

content.title: Nvidia Stock Slips Below $200. Competition Fears Are Growing.
content.summary: Nvidia  slid on Thursday as the initial boost from  Micron’s  blowout earnings report faded, giving way to deeper anxieties about artificial-intelligence stocks.  Extending its longest losing streak since May, Nvidia stock fell for the fourth day straight, dropping 1.8% to $195.47.  As Barron’s has written, Nvidia looked to be establishing a trading range with a floor of roughly $200, after breaking through that level in April.

headline: Mag 7 Stocks Shed Nearly $3 Trillion This Month
summary: A massive rotation at the expense of big AI spenders has the Magnificent Seven on pace to shed $3 trillion this month.  Collectively, Tesla, Apple, Microsoft, Meta Platforms, Nvidia, Amazon.com, and Alphabet have lost $2.99 trillion in market cap so far in June, according to Dow Jones Market Data.  The Roundhill Magnificent Seven ETF is on pace for its worst month on record.

content.title: Mag 7 Stocks Shed Nearly $3 Trillion This Month
content.summary: A massive rotation at the expense of big AI spenders has the Magnificent Seven on pace to shed $3 trillion this month.  Collectively, Tesla, Apple, Microsoft, Meta Platforms, Nvidia, Amazon.com, and Alphabet have lost $2.99 trillion in market cap so far in June, according to Dow Jones Market Data.  The Roundhill Magnificent Seven ETF is on pace for its worst month on record.
